Genesis: HOOP Implementation
description:
# Genesis: HOOP Implementation

**Tied to plan:** `/home/coding/HOOP/docs/plan/plan.md` (23 sections)

## What HOOP is

A single Rust daemon that runs on a long-lived operator host and serves as the human-facing interface to a multi-project NEEDLE worker fleet. HOOP reads artifacts across projects (beads, events, conversations, files, costs, capacity) and writes exactly one kind of record: beads, via `br create`, when the operator or the human-interface agent drafts new work.

HOOP's unique position is the *join* across `projects × Stitches × conversations × files × cost × time`. Its value is making that join legible to a human operator.

## Core invariants (do not break)

1. **`br create` is HOOP's only write.** No bead close/update/release. No tmux control. No worker lifecycle. No capacity enforcement.
2. **Events are authoritative; projections derived.** Never cache to disk what can be rebuilt from events.
3. **Liveness = process, never file.** `kill -0 pid && !stopped_record`.
4. **Humans work in Stitches.** Bead IDs appear only in expert/audit views.
5. **Lazy context for the agent.** Thin index by default; tool calls for details on demand.
6. **Stitches descend into obscurity by recency; they do not close.**
7. **LLM-agnostic agent.** Adapter config matches NEEDLE's; switchable without code changes.
8. **Security inherited from environment.** No custom auth; SSH + Tailscale are the trust root.

## Phase checklist

- [x] Phase 0: Foundation (docs scaffolding, notes, plan)
- [ ] Phase 1: Single-host daemon, one workspace, read-only (v0.1)
- [ ] Phase 2: Multi-project observability + marquee features (v0.2)
- [ ] Phase 3: File browser + artifact preview + multimodal (v0.3)
- [ ] Phase 4: Bead creation interface (v0.4)
- [ ] Phase 5: Human-interface agent (v0.5)
- [ ] Phase 6: Operational polish (v0.6)
- [ ] Phase 7: Multi-operator (v1.0)

## Cross-cutting concerns

- §12 Onboarding & documentation
- §13 Security model (inherited from environment)
- §14 Testing strategy (with `testrepo/` dummy fixture)
- §15 Backups & disaster recovery (S3-compatible, B2 default)
- §16 Self-observability (Prometheus metrics, `/debug/state`)
- §17 Configuration precedence & hot-reload (config.yml + env vars)
- §18 Privacy & redaction
- §19 Multi-operator concurrency (phase 7)
- §20 Schema evolution (SemVer)
- §21 Mobile UX (responsive from day one)
- §22 Extensibility (skills / scripts / notes / prompts)

## Vocabulary (canonical — do not reintroduce removed terms)

- **Project** — logical unit, contains one or more Workspaces
- **Workspace** — single repo with its own `.beads/`
- **Stitch** — single conversation within a project (operator / dictated / worker / ad-hoc)
- **Pattern** — optional cross-project grouping of Stitches toward a goal
- **Bead** — NEEDLE's execution unit; HOOP abstracts it
- **Human-interface agent** — persistent conversational role (was "Mayor" — removed)
- **Reflection Ledger** — learned-rules store

Removed vocabulary (do not reintroduce): Mayor, polecat, swarm (as term), convoy, Gas Town lineage.

## References

- Plan: `docs/plan/plan.md`
- Onboarding: `README.md`
- LLM guide: `AGENTS.md`
- Prior-art notes: `docs/notes/{reference-feature-inventory,architecture-patterns,interop-with-needle,orchestrator-problems-and-solutions}.md`

## Closing criteria

Close this genesis bead when all seven phase epics close with success criteria met and public README published (phase 7 v1.0 target).

title: X: Security model (§13)
description:
# Cross-cutting: Security model

**Plan section:** §13

## Posture
**Security inherited from the environment.** HOOP does not implement its own authentication layer. It binds to localhost (SSH-tunnel accessed) or the Tailscale interface. Whoever can reach the port is authenticated via the operator's pre-existing trust root (SSH keys, Tailscale ACLs, OS user).

## What HOOP still owns
- Path-traversal hardening on every filesystem operation
- Attachment sanitization (SVG XSS, PDF metadata, content-sniffed file types)
- Audit-log append-only + daily hash chain for tamper evidence
- Secrets scanning on attachments and transcripts (details in §18)
- MCP socket on Unix domain socket with same user:group (not TCP)
- Regex-validate every wire ID before filesystem use (`^bd-[a-z0-9]+$`, etc.)

## Explicit non-authentication patterns
- No password prompts
- No session cookies
- No JWT verification
- No IdP integration

## References
- Plan §13 Security model
- Plan §18 Privacy & redaction (related)
- Notes: orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §K

## Closing criteria
- All path-derived operations use canonical allowlist matching
- Audit log hash chain verifiable
- Secrets scanner integrated into attachment pipeline
- MCP socket permissions tested
- No custom auth code path exists

title: Path-traversal hardening: canonicalize + allowlist every wire-derived path
description:
## Task
Every filesystem operation whose path derives from a wire ID canonicalizes and matches against a pre-computed allowlist of acceptable roots (project workspace, project `.beads/`, attachment dir).

## Acceptance
- Regex-validate wire IDs before use (`^bd-[a-z0-9]+$`, `^stitch-[a-z0-9]+$`, `^[a-z]+$` for worker names)
- `realpath` resolution then prefix-match against allowlist
- Rejection returns 400 with safe error (don't echo bad path)
- Test fixtures include 10 path-traversal attack vectors; all rejected

## Plan reference
§13 Security, §K2 from orchestrator-problems-and-solutions

title: Central ID validators module (bead_id, stitch_id, pattern_id, worker_name regex gates)
description:
## Task
Centralize: `validate_bead_id` (`^bd-[a-z0-9]+$`), `validate_stitch_id`, `validate_pattern_id`, `validate_worker_name` (`^[a-z]+$`). Apply at every HTTP handler and filesystem boundary. Wire IDs never hit `fs::` paths without passing through a validator.

## Acceptance
- Single module owns all ID regexes
- Unit tests cover: leading dash, unicode, dots, whitespace, empty, too-long
- Misuse caught in code review (lint rule or compile-time type)

## Plan reference
§13 (paragraph 4: regex-validate IDs before any filesystem use)

title: Attachment sanitization: SVG strip scripts, PDF metadata validate, content-sniff file types
description:
## Task
Every uploaded attachment goes through sanitization:
- SVG: strip `<script>`, event handlers, embedded foreign content
- PDF: validate metadata fields against known-safe schema
- All: content-sniff the type (magic bytes), not the extension
- Reject mismatch between claimed and actual type

## Acceptance
- SVG with script tag rejected with clear error
- Mismatch (claimed PNG, actual EXE) rejected
- PDF with embedded JS sanitized to no-JS version
- Tests cover each attack class

## Plan reference
§13 Security attachment sanitization
close_reason: Attachment sanitization (§13) already fully implemented in commits 8ab6b58 and b497df3. Covers: SVG sanitizer strips script/foreignObject/event-handlers/external-href/DOCTYPE; PDF sanitizer neutralises embedded JS via same-length byte replacement preserving xref offsets; content-type sniffing via infer crate with mismatch rejection; sidecar .meta.json metadata persistence. 92 tests across all attack classes passing.

title: Content-type sniffing (magic bytes) for every uploaded attachment
description:
## Task
Every upload classified by leading-byte signature via `infer` or `tree_magic`. Mismatch between declared extension and actual content → rejection with clear error. Store both declared and sniffed types.

## Acceptance
- Test fixtures: PNG-claimed-as-JPG, ZIP-claimed-as-SVG, EXE-claimed-as-PDF → all rejected
- Matching cases pass through (legit PNG, legit PDF)
- Sniffed type persisted in attachment metadata

## Plan reference
§13 (file extensions verified by content sniffing)

title: SVG attachment sanitizer: strip script, event handlers, external refs
description:
## Task
Every SVG upload passes through sanitizer (e.g. `ammonia` + XML walker). Strips `<script>`, `on*` attributes, `xlink:href` pointing to http(s), `<foreignObject>` content. Produces safe-render SVG.

## Acceptance
- XSS-in-SVG corpus neutralized
- Legit-SVG corpus renders unchanged (visual regression)
- Sanitized and original both retained (original marked unsafe)

## Plan reference
§13 (SVG stripped of scripts)

title: Audit log hash chain: each row hashes prior row content
description:
## Task
actions table carries `hash_prev` and `hash_self`. Each row's hash includes prior row's hash, providing tamper evidence for post-hoc review.

## Acceptance
- Hash function: SHA-256
- First row's prev = genesis constant
- Verification command: `hoop audit verify` walks chain, reports tampering
- Backup manifest includes final hash as integrity anchor

## Plan reference
§13 Security audit hash chain

title: MCP socket permissions: Unix domain socket, same user:group, not TCP
description:
## Task
The MCP server (phase 5) binds to a UDS at `~/.hoop/mcp.sock`. Mode 0600. Only processes running as the same OS user can connect.

## Acceptance
- Socket mode verified on startup
- Binding fails cleanly if mode cannot be set
- Zero TCP exposure for MCP
- Smoke test: another user can't connect

## Plan reference
§13 Security (MCP on UDS), §6 Phase 5 (MCP server)

title: Automated dependency + security scanning in CI (cargo audit, npm audit, trivy)
description:
## Task
Add CI steps (in Argo Workflow) for:
- `cargo audit` — RUSTSEC advisories
- `pnpm audit` — npm package vulns
- `trivy fs .` — secrets + vuln scan on source tree
- `trivy image` on built Docker image

Failures block releases (not merges — allow patch). Weekly cron updates advisories.

## Why
Operational security — transitive dependencies rot. Prior-art: every orchestrator repo eventually hit this.

## Acceptance
- CI steps functional
- Baseline run captures current state; new vulns surface in PR comments
- Docs/operations.md has remediation flow

## Plan reference
§13 Security; §22.8 script extensibility audit

title: X: Testing strategy & testrepo fixture (§14)
description:
# Cross-cutting: Testing strategy

**Plan section:** §14

## Foundation: the `testrepo/` dummy project

The HOOP repo ships a realistic dummy workspace at `testrepo/`. Every integration and E2E test operates against `testrepo/` — no live NEEDLE, no live CLI, no live LLM required.

Contents:
- Rust crate + docs + config (~500 files)
- Pre-populated `.beads/` with synthetic beads in known states
- Pre-recorded CLI session JSONL per adapter (Claude / Codex / OpenCode / Gemini / Aider) with proper tag prefixes
- Canned `events.jsonl` and `heartbeats.jsonl`
- Example attachments (image, audio, video) for multimodal tests
- `br` stub binary checked in, records calls without requiring a real install

## Test layers
1. Unit — pure functions (event parsers, tag extraction, path canonicalization, cost math, capacity arithmetic, Stitch status derivation, schema version negotiation)
2. Integration — daemon boots, tails fixtures, serves WS, test client drives + asserts
3. E2E — full `hoop init` + project reg + Stitch creation + `br create` + UI assertions
4. Property (`proptest`) — invariants: events in-order, derived status monotonic, replay-from-disk equals live
5. Load — 20 projects × 5 workers × 200 beads synthetic; UI responsiveness, memory, fan-out
6. UI (Playwright) — headless browser vs production binary; desktop + mobile viewports

## Deliberate non-test
- Real `br` version matrix (pin minimum, one version)
- Real LLM (use golden transcripts; opt-in suite for releases)
- Real Tailscale (use localhost in CI)

## References
- Plan §14 Testing strategy
- Plan §10 Milestones (phase success criteria → tests)

## Closing criteria
- `testrepo/` committed and populated with realistic fixtures
- Every phase's success criteria translate to automated tests running green in CI
- Load test harness runs in CI; budget violations block merge

title: Build testrepo/ fixture: realistic file tree + synthetic .beads/ + recorded CLI sessions
description:
## Task
Create the testrepo/ dummy workspace. Contents:
- ~500 files: Rust crate + docs + config
- Pre-populated `.beads/` with synthetic beads in known states (some open, claimed, closed with commit trailers, failed)
- Pre-recorded CLI session JSONL per adapter (Claude/Codex/OpenCode/Gemini/Aider) with proper `[needle:...]` prefixes
- Canned `events.jsonl` and `heartbeats.jsonl`
- Example attachments (image, audio, video)
- `br` stub binary that records calls

## Acceptance
- `testrepo/` committed to HOOP repo
- All integration tests pass against testrepo
- Fixture regeneration script documented
- Size bounded (<50MB)

## Plan reference
§14 Testing strategy

title: Stub br binary in testrepo/bin/ (records calls, emulates create, reads fixtures)
description:
## Task
Minimal `br-stub` at `testrepo/bin/br`. Accepts all read verbs against fixture JSON. Records `create` calls for integration-test assertions. Avoids real br dependency in CI.

## Acceptance
- PATH-installable for tests
- Correct exit codes for all verbs
- Records to `testrepo/.stub-log.jsonl` for test inspection

## Plan reference
§14.2 integration tests

title: Invariant test: no-auto-submit (create_stitch always routes through draft queue)
description:
## Task
Integration test asserting every `create_stitch` path enters the draft queue. No `br create` happens without explicit operator approval.

## Why
Plan principle 10 (read-first default) is the highest-severity invariant HOOP asserts. An accidental `--force` flag or `auto: true` reaching mainline would silently write beads.

## Acceptance
- Call `create_stitch` with every combination of flags
- Assert draft row created in fleet.db
- Assert NO bead created in `.beads/beads.db` until `approve_draft` fires
- CI test fails merge if path bypasses preview

## Plan reference
§3.10

title: Integration test: HOOP-dies-nothing-notices (kill daemon mid-run, NEEDLE continues, restart replays <5s)
description:
## Task
Verify principle 9 with a live test. Spawn stub NEEDLE against testrepo; kill `hoop serve`; assert NEEDLE still claims + closes; restart hoop; assert UI state rebuilt from disk in <5s.

## Why
This is the "HOOP is a convenience, not a dependency" contract. Without a test it's a claim.

## Acceptance
- Stub NEEDLE fixture runs against testrepo/
- Mid-run SIGKILL of hoop serve
- events.jsonl continues to grow during HOOP's absence
- Restart: projections correct within 5s
- Repeated 10x to catch flakes

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 1 success, §3.9

title: Static audit: forbid projection-shaped file writes (worker_status.json, fleet_state.json)
description:
## Task
CI check fails build if any code path writes files matching `*_status.json`, `live-*.json`, `fleet_state.*`. Explicit allowlist for exceptions.

## Why
Plan principle 1: events authoritative, projections derived. Prior art ran into "51 wedged workers" (§A4) because cached projections outlived reality.

## Acceptance
- grep/trybuild-based CI check
- Synthetic PR adding `worker_state.json` write fails the check
- Allowlist documented with rationale per exception

## Plan reference
§3.1, notes/orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §A3-A4

title: Unit test suite: pure functions (parsers, tag extract, canonicalization, cost math, status derivation)
description:
## Task
`cargo test` unit tests for every pure function identified in plan §14.2.

## Acceptance
- Coverage >80% on pure-function modules
- Runs in <60s
- CI-gated (fails merge if coverage drops)

## Plan reference
§14.2 Test layers, unit tests

title: Integration test harness: daemon boot → tail testrepo/ → test client assertions
description:
## Task
Spin up HOOP against testrepo/, drive WS + REST interactions with a test client, assert state projections are correct.

## Acceptance
- Each phase's success criteria translates to integration-test cases
- Tests are hermetic (no flakiness)
- Runs in <5min

## Plan reference
§14.2 Test layers, integration

title: Property tests (proptest): event ordering invariants, status monotonicity, replay = live
description:
## Task
Property-based tests over event streams and derived state. Key invariants:
- Event tailer never emits out-of-order events
- Derived status functions monotonic where expected
- Replay from disk produces state equal to live

## Acceptance
- proptest crate integrated
- 3+ invariants defined
- Shrinking + seed stability documented

## Plan reference
§14.2 Test layers, property

title: Liveness-never-from-file property test (proptest: liveness = pid_alive && !stopped_record)
description:
## Task
Proptest: generate (pid_alive, stopped_record) permutations; assert derived liveness equals `pid_alive && !stopped_record`. Also assert derivation never reads a file named `*_status*`.

## Why
Principle 2: liveness = process, never file. §M6 prior-art: "parent died without stopped.json → dashboards show running forever."

## Acceptance
- proptest covers all 4 truth-table combinations + edge cases
- Derivation trace asserts no cached-file read
- Test fails if someone adds a cached-liveness shortcut

## Plan reference
§3.2, notes/orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §A4, §M6

title: Playwright UI tests: desktop + mobile viewports, visual regression
description:
## Task
Headless browser tests against the production binary serving the embedded client. Desktop (1920×1080) and mobile (Pixel 6 viewport 412×915) suites. Visual regressions block CI.

## Acceptance
- Each major panel has smoke tests
- Mobile-specific flows tested (dictation button, Morning Brief cards)
- Visual diffs tolerated within threshold; new baselines explicitly approved

## Plan reference
§14.2 UI tests, §21 Mobile UX

title: Atomic .tmp + rename invariant audit across all HOOP write paths
description:
## Task
Audit every filesystem write in hoop-daemon (audit log, fleet.db backups, attachments, upload finalize, config persistence). Confirm all use write-tmp → fsync → rename. Crash-injection test asserts partial files never surface.

## Acceptance
- Grep/lint rule prevents direct writes (only via atomic-write helper)
- Crash-injection test at 5 points; no partial files
- Helper function `atomic_write_file()` centralized

## Plan reference
§3 principle 6, notes/architecture-patterns §F

title: Load-test driver: synthetic 20×5×200 activity generator vs daemon
description:
## Task
Driver generates concurrent synthetic event streams (20 projects × 5 workers × 200 beads, plausible claim/close cadence) and drives the daemon. Asserts UI responsiveness budget, memory ceiling, WS fan-out lag.

## Acceptance
- Generator configurable
- Runs in CI (flagged optional for PR, required for release)
- Feeds into hoop-ttb.7.11 performance budget verification

## Plan reference
§14.2 bullet 5

title: Golden-transcripts corpus per adapter (Claude, Codex, OpenCode, Gemini, Aider)
description:
## Task
Check in `testrepo/golden-transcripts/<adapter>/<version>/*.jsonl` with expected-parsed-output fixtures. Parser regressions produce visible diffs in CI. Each adapter has ≥ 3 scenarios: simple turn, tool-heavy turn, failure turn.

## Acceptance
- Corpus committed (bounded size, <10MB)
- Adapter parser changes force golden refresh
- Regression test runs on every PR

## Plan reference
§14.3 golden transcripts

title: Core-invariant test suite CI tie (zero-write, compile-fail, panic-isolation, draft-queue)
description:
## Task
Tests exist (`zero_write_invariant.rs`, `compile_fail_create_only.rs`, `panic_isolation.rs`, `draft_queue_invariants.rs`, `create_only_stub.rs`). Ensure:
- All run on every PR
- Failures block merge
- Per-test documentation links back to the plan principle it enforces

## Acceptance
- CI workflow explicitly runs each test
- Test names map to plan sections in README or AGENTS.md
- Failure output includes pointer to the invariant being violated

## Plan reference
§3 principles 6, 7, 8, 9; §6 Phase 1 + 4 invariants

title: Lint: no shared /g regex with captures across async boundaries
description:
## Task
Clippy rule or lint check that flags any `Regex` stored in global state (lazy_static, OnceCell) when combined with concurrent `captures_iter` use.

## Why
§F3: shared stateful regexes produce nondeterministic parses under load. Catastrophic for audit correctness.

## Acceptance
- Lint rule identifies dangerous pattern
- CI fails on violation
- Documented exception mechanism for stateless uses

## Plan reference
notes/orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §F3

title: X: Backups & disaster recovery (§15)
description:
# Cross-cutting: Backups & disaster recovery

**Plan section:** §15

## Scope
Everything HOOP owns under `~/.hoop/`:
- `config.yml` — non-secret configuration
- `projects.yaml` — project registry
- `fleet.db` — audit, Stitches, Patterns, Reflection Ledger
- `attachments/` — audio, images, video, screen captures
- `skills/`, `scripts/`, `notes/`, `prompts/` — operator extensions
- `templates/` — Stitch templates

Explicitly out of scope: bead state (`br`'s job), NEEDLE state (separate), CLI session files (each CLI's), git state (git's).

## Target
Configurable **S3-compatible endpoint** (B2 default, AWS S3, MinIO, Garage, any S3 API). Credentials via env vars; endpoint + bucket in `config.yml`. Matches existing environment's ARMOR-encrypted-B2 pattern.

## What gets backed up
- `fleet.db` daily via `VACUUM INTO` snapshot → compressed + optionally `age`-encrypted → upload
- Attachments incremental sync (only new/changed since last success)
- Config files on every change plus daily
- Each run writes a `manifest.json` with schema version + piece list

## Restore
- `hoop restore --from s3://<bucket>/<prefix>/<snapshot-id>`
- Idempotent; precondition: `hoop serve` stopped
- Moves existing `~/.hoop/` aside for rollback before restore

## DR scenarios covered
- Disk death
- `fleet.db` corruption
- Accidental deletion
- Host migration

## References
- Plan §15
- Memory: `project_armor_backup_strategy.md` (ARMOR encrypted S3 proxy for B2)

## Closing criteria
- Backup runs on schedule; credentials validated
- Restore from recent snapshot produces identical state (verified)
- Documentation covers all four DR scenarios
- age encryption works with key in env var

title: Backup config schema + env-var credential resolver (S3-compatible)
description:
## Task
Wire the `backup:` config section: endpoint, bucket, prefix, schedule (cron), retention_days, optional encryption. Credentials come from env vars: `HOOP_BACKUP_ACCESS_KEY_ID`, `HOOP_BACKUP_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`, `HOOP_BACKUP_AGE_KEY`. Never in YAML, never in audit log.

## Acceptance
- Config section parses and validates
- Missing credentials → daemon starts but disables backups with clear log
- `hoop-schema/schemas/backup_config.json` up to date

## Plan reference
§15.2
close_reason: §15.2 complete: Backup config schema + env-var credential resolver. Verified parsing (endpoint, bucket, prefix, schedule, retention_days, encryption) with env-var-only credentials. Added redacting Debug impl for BackupCredentials. Schema up to date. 8/8 tests pass.

title: Daily fleet.db snapshot pipeline: VACUUM INTO → zstd → age (optional) → S3
description:
## Task
Implement the backup pipeline. `VACUUM INTO` a temp snapshot, zstd-compress, optionally age-encrypt, multipart upload to S3 with retry. Metrics record duration and size.

## Acceptance
- Daily cron fires per config schedule
- Failure: exponential backoff, max 3 retries, then alert
- Encryption skipped cleanly when no age key set
- Metrics `hoop_backup_last_success_timestamp`, `hoop_backup_last_size_bytes` updated

## Plan reference
§15.3, §16.6
close_reason: Backup pipeline already implemented and committed (665b2ea). VACUUM INTO -> zstd -> age (optional) -> S3 PUT with exponential backoff (max 3 retries). Cron scheduler with at-most-once-per-day guard. All 16 unit tests passing.

title: Incremental attachments sync (diff vs last manifest)
description:
## Task
Attachments grow fast; full sync every run is wasteful. Each run compares current attachments tree against prior manifest and uploads only new/changed files.

## Acceptance
- Manifest records per-file sha256 + size + mtime
- Diff computes add/change/delete sets
- Deletes preserved (tombstone, not remove) for N days

## Plan reference
§15.3
close_reason: Already implemented in commit 524099b (S15.3). All acceptance criteria met: manifest tracks sha256+size+mtime per file, diff computes add/change/delete sets with mtime+size shortcut, deleted files preserved as tombstones for configurable retention days. Fully tested with 15+ unit tests.

title: Backup manifest.json format + schema version pinning
description:
## Task
Each backup writes a manifest.json tying pieces together. Must include the HOOP schema version at snapshot time so restore can detect newer-than-current snapshots.

## Schema
```
{
  "snapshot_id": "...",
  "created_at": "...",
  "schema_version": "1.4.2",
  "fleet_db_key": "fleet.db.zst[.age]",
  "attachments_manifest_key": "attachments.manifest.json",
  "encryption": "none|age",
  "hoop_version": "..."
}
```

## Acceptance
- Manifest uploaded atomically (last, after all pieces)
- Restore validates manifest before destructive action
- Newer schema_version rejected per §20.1

## Plan reference
§15.3, §20.1

title: docs/operations.md — DR runbook for all four scenarios
description:
## Task
Runbook for disk death, fleet.db corruption, accidental deletion, host migration. Each scenario: exact command, expected duration, pitfalls.

## Acceptance
- All four §15.5 scenarios covered
- Tested end-to-end against a test snapshot
- Linked from README + AGENTS.md

## Plan reference
§15.5

title: hoop restore: newer-than-current snapshot rejection + rollback on failure
description:
## Task
Harden hoop-ttb.7.7's restore command: manifest.schema_version > binary's → refuse. On mid-flight failure, restore the pre-move `~/.hoop/` backup aside.

## Acceptance
- Test: restore a synthetic newer-version snapshot → clear rejection diagnostic
- Test: restore that fails after moving original → rollback puts original back
- Daemon refuses to start if restore state is inconsistent

## Plan reference
§15.4, §20.1

title: X: Self-observability — metrics & /debug (§16)
description:
# Cross-cutting: Self-observability

**Plan section:** §16

## Why
A daemon that reads everyone's files, shells to `br`, spawns Whisper, and drives an LLM has many internal states. Without metrics, operators can't diagnose HOOP itself.

## Surfaces
- `/metrics` — Prometheus-format
- `/debug/state` — JSON endpoint with fleet roster, open Stitches, agent sessions, every pid, every WS client, config hash, backup timestamps
- Local-only binding by default

## Metric categories (from plan §16)
16.1 Operational — uptime, memory, FDs, panics, errors
16.2 Event ingestion — tailer lag per project/adapter, heartbeat freshness, unknown-event counter
16.3 WebSocket/HTTP — client count, broadcast lag, HTTP route stats
16.4 Bead/Stitch — `br` success/latency, Stitch/bead creation rate, audit append rate, orphan count
16.5 Agent/AI — turn duration, tool call success, token usage, Whisper duration, Reflection proposal/approval rates
16.6 Storage — db size, WAL, attachments, migration duration, backup timestamps
16.7 Business — cost per Stitch, anomaly alerts, dedup hits, capacity warnings

## References
- Plan §16

title: X: Configuration & hot-reload (§17)
description:
# Cross-cutting: Configuration & hot-reload

**Plan section:** §17

## Precedence (deterministic)
```
CLI flags > env vars > config.yml > compiled defaults
```

## Layout
- `~/.hoop/config.yml` — non-secret configuration (the single source of truth for almost everything)
- Env vars — secrets only (API keys, S3 credentials, age encryption keys); never in audit log or default-verbosity logs
- CLI flags — bootstrap only (`hoop serve`'s config path, socket path, log level)

## Config.yml sections
- `agent:` — adapter/model/rate limits/cost cap
- `projects_file:` — projects.yaml path
- `backup:` — endpoint/bucket/prefix/schedule/retention
- `ui:` — theme, archive-after-days, default sort
- `voice:` — Whisper model, hotkey, max record seconds
- `agent_extensions:` — skills/scripts/notes/prompts dirs (§22)
- `metrics:` — `/metrics` enable + port
- `audit:` — retention, hash-chain on/off
- `reflection:` — detection thresholds, proposal auto-archive

## Hot-reload
**Everything reloads without restart**, including projects.yaml, agent adapter config, backup schedule, log verbosity, UI preferences.

Exceptions (restart required):
- Socket paths, listen addresses
- `fleet.db` location

## Validation
Any edit is schema-validated BEFORE apply. Invalid edits rejected loudly (UI banner + metric + log); old config stays running. **A bad config never takes down the daemon.**

## References
- Plan §17

## Closing criteria
- All config sections hot-reload without restart
- Schema validation catches all type/bound errors
- Precedence rules implemented and documented
- Bad-config test: edit-to-invalid leaves daemon running correctly

JSON Schema in `hoop-schema/schemas/hoop_config.json`. Rust types via typify. TS types via json-schema-to-typescript.

## Acceptance

- Schema version tagged on the config itself (schema_version on config root)
- Completeness verified against plan §17.3

Schema version tagged on config root. Rust types via typify, TS types via json-schema-to-typescript. Round-trip equality tests pass for full and minimal configs. All 13 hoop-schema tests green.

title: X: Privacy & redaction (§18)
description:
# Cross-cutting: Privacy & redaction

**Plan section:** §18

## Attack surface
- Dictation (audio + Whisper transcript)
- Screen captures (frames + audio + transcript)
- Attachments (images, videos, PDFs)
- Agent conversations (operator speech + file contents)
- CLI session tailers (reading JSONL written by CLIs)

## Defenses
- **Secrets scanner** on every attachment upload: API keys (Stripe/OpenAI/Anthropic/AWS/GitHub patterns), env-var-looking leaks, high-entropy strings with context-aware exclusions, email PII
- **Warning not blocking** — operator sees what was flagged and chooses redact-in-place / redact-and-rewind / proceed. Nothing silent.
- **Transcript redaction with word-timestamps** — redact word in transcript + mute matching seconds in audio, atomically
- **Read-side session tailer** — HOOP's projections go through redaction filter; raw CLI JSONL files never mutated
- **Log hygiene** — WARN default; INFO/DEBUG also redacted

## Operator control
- `config.yml` policy: pattern sets, entropy threshold, action (warn / redact / reject)
- Per-project override (strict mode for customer-data projects)
- Audit trail of every redaction

## What HOOP cannot prevent
CLI agents write their own session JSONL; secrets there are in the CLI's native cache, not HOOP's responsibility to clean.

## References
- Plan §18
- Plan §13 (security model — related)

## Closing criteria
- Secrets scanner catches common token formats in test fixtures
- Word-level audio muting verified end-to-end
- Log lines redacted at default verbosity
- Per-project policy override tested
- Audit trail for redactions complete

title: Secrets scanner for attachments and transcripts
description:
## Task
Pre-storage scan of every attachment and transcript. Detects:
- Common secret patterns (Stripe, OpenAI, Anthropic, AWS, GitHub, JWT)
- Env-var-style leaks (`OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...`)
- High-entropy strings >N bits, with context-aware exclusions (git SHAs fine, other high-entropy suspicious)
- Email addresses per operator-configured PII patterns

## Behavior
**Flag, not block**. Operator sees warning banner listing findings, chooses: redact-in-place, redact-and-rewind, proceed.

## Acceptance
- Synthetic secrets in fixtures caught correctly
- False positive rate <5% on `testrepo/` content
- Redaction is atomic (transcript word + audio timestamp mute)
- Config: policy set (pattern library, action defaults) in `config.yml`

## Plan reference
§18 Privacy & redaction

title: Unify client-side secretsScanner.ts with backend scanner (single pattern set from config.yml)
description:
## Task
Reverse gap: `hoop-ui/web/src/components/secretsScanner.ts` exists. Unify with backend scanner. Single pattern set sourced from config.yml. Client uses for pre-upload warning; backend does authoritative scan.

## Why
Two scanners will disagree; duplicate logic drifts. Single source of truth = single behavior.

## Acceptance
- Config.yml exposes pattern set; backend + client read same file or shared schema
- Client warns pre-upload; backend blocks/redacts authoritatively
- Tests confirm parity on fixture secrets

## Plan reference
§18

title: Transcript word-level audio muting on redaction
description:
## Task
When the operator redacts a word from a Whisper transcript, mute the matching audio timestamps. Both operations atomic.

## Acceptance
- Word-level timestamps from Whisper preserved
- Audio mute implemented via ffmpeg filter or direct byte manipulation
- Transcript shows `[REDACTED]`; audio plays silence for that span
- Reversible only from audit log (no accidental undo)

## Plan reference
§18.2 Transcript redaction

title: Read-side redaction filter on session-tailer output + agent-forwarded content
description:
## Task
Claude/Codex/OpenCode/Gemini/Aider session JSONL content runs through secrets-scanner redaction filter before HOOP UI projections OR MCP tool responses. Per-line cache avoids re-scanning.

## Why
CRITICAL — HOOP's projection layer is the leak surface. Without this filter, a token accidentally typed into a Claude session could surface in HOOP's conversation viewer or agent context.

## Acceptance
- Synthetic secret in a test session JSONL → shown as `[REDACTED]` in UI + in agent tool response
- Raw CLI JSONL files never modified
- Redaction cache evicts on file rotation

## Plan reference
§18.3 Read-side redaction

title: Per-project redaction policy override in projects.yaml
description:

Add `redaction:` sub-block to project entries in projects.yaml: pattern sets, action (`warn` / `redact` / `reject`). Overrides global config.yml policy per project.

## Acceptance
- Schema updated; hot-reload supported

title: X: Multi-operator concurrency (§19) — primarily phase 7
description:
# Cross-cutting: Multi-operator concurrency

**Plan section:** §19

## Scope
The specifics of two+ operators using the same HOOP concurrently. Primarily lands in phase 7 but shapes earlier data-model decisions.

## Specifics
1. **Draft concurrency** — drafts are server-persisted from the moment the form opens, not just on save. Two concurrent drafts both land.
2. **Reflection Ledger concurrency** — proposals deduplicated on create (content-hash). Approvals are single-operator actions.
3. **Agent session ownership** — one session per operator. No shared agents.
4. **Presence** — optional per-project and per-Stitch presence indicators, privacy-toggleable.
5. **Conflict resolution** — not a lock; two Stitches on the same workspace both land. Already-Started Detection (marquee #8) warns operators if they're duplicating.

## References
- Plan §19
- Plan §6 Phase 7
- Plan §13 (identity via Tailscale whois)

## Closing criteria
- Two concurrent drafts don't clobber each other
- Presence indicators optional and privacy-respecting
- Proposal dedup tested with same-content-hash collisions
- Per-operator agent sessions isolated correctly

title: X: Schema evolution (SemVer) (§20)
description:
# Cross-cutting: Schema evolution (SemVer)

**Plan section:** §20

## Policy
All durable records carry `schema_version: "X.Y.Z"`. Semantic Versioning applies:

- **Major** — breaking, no backwards compat. One-way migration. Upgrade-gate: `hoop migrate --from-1 --confirm` required.
- **Minor** — additive, backwards-compat. Old records read correctly; new records may have fields old readers ignore.
- **Patch** — bug fix, no shape change.

## Deprecation window
- Minor deprecations readable at least one full minor version after introduction.
- Major deprecations are one-way; operator consciously accepts at upgrade gate.

## Interaction with `br`
HOOP pins a minimum compatible `br` major version. A `br` major bump triggers HOOP's compatibility audit.

## Backup manifest includes schema version
`hoop restore` refuses newer-than-current snapshots.

## References
- Plan §20
- Plan §2.1 (`br` dependency, version pinning)

## Closing criteria
- Every record type emits `schema_version`
- Minor deprecation path documented (one full version window)
- Major upgrade gate implemented with explicit flag
- Restore refuses newer snapshots
- Schema source of truth in `hoop-schema/` with Rust + TS codegen

# Cross-cutting: Mobile UX

**Plan section:** §21

- Multi-project time-range dashboards (compact summary only)

title: X: Extensibility — skills, scripts, notes, prompts (§22)
description:
# Cross-cutting: Extensibility (skills / scripts / notes / prompts)

**Plan section:** §22

## Philosophy
HOOP is extensible without code changes. Four directory-based plugin types modeled on OpenClaw/Hermes and Claude Code's skills pattern.

## Plugin types

### Skills — `~/.hoop/skills/<name>/`
- Custom agent tools (directory with `manifest.yml` + executable `run` + optional README)
- Auto-discovered on startup and directory watch
- Agent's tool belt augmented dynamically
- Examples: `lookup-deploy-status`, `fetch-prod-logs-summary`

### Scripts — `~/.hoop/scripts/<name>`
- Executable files, any language
- Triggered: operator-invoked (UI button or `hoop script run`), event-triggered (manifest), scheduled (cron)
- Full operator privileges (HOOP user)
- **This is the escape hatch for external integrations** (replaces non-goal #13's forbidden built-in integrations)

### Notes — `~/.hoop/notes/<name>.md` (also per-project `<project>/.hoop/notes/`)
- Plain markdown; agent reads via `read_note(name)`
- Project glossaries, team conventions, reference material

### Prompts — `~/.hoop/prompts/<name>.md`
- Reusable prompt bodies, referenced by `@prompt:<name>`
- Parameter substitution: `{{project}}`, `{{file}}`, `{{stitch}}`

## Discovery & hot-reload
All four file-watched. New skill → agent's next turn has the new tool. Same for scripts/notes/prompts.

## Security
Skills and scripts run with HOOP user's privileges. No sandboxing. Operator owns their extensions. Audit log records every skill invocation with arguments.

## Sharing
Flat files → plain git clone into the dir. Tarballs. Community registry if anyone builds one (not HOOP's problem).

## References
- Plan §22

## Closing criteria
- All four plugin dirs hot-reloadable
- Skill manifest schema documented with examples
- Script triggers (manual/event/cron) all functional
- Audit log captures every skill invocation
- Quickstart example: one working skill, one script, one note, one prompt in the docs

title: Phase 1: Single-host daemon, one workspace, read-only (v0.1)
description:
# Phase 1: Single-host daemon, one workspace, read-only (v0.1)

**Version target:** v0.1 | **Timeline:** +4 weeks from phase start

## Goal
HOOP runs on an EX44-class host as a pure observer of one workspace. Serves a web UI showing bead state, worker liveness, conversations, events. Zero write paths.

## Why this phase exists
Prove the full end-to-end loop works (event tailing → join → UI) before adding breadth. A broken single-project loop doesn't get better by adding more projects.

## What "done" looks like
- Rust binary builds, runs as systemd user service, serves UI on Tailscale hostname
- Observes an existing NEEDLE fleet without affecting it
- Restart → reconstructs state entirely from disk in <5s for 500 beads
- Zero silent drops in any parser
- Every bead visible in the UI; worker transcripts show bead IDs via tag-join

## Success criteria (from plan §6 Phase 1)
- HOOP runs alongside a NEEDLE fleet without affecting it
- Killing HOOP does nothing to the fleet
- Every bead visible with worker transcripts joined
- Zero silent drops — unknown events appear in diagnostic panel

## Non-goals
- Multi-project (phase 2)
- Any write path (phase 4)
- Cost/capacity panels (phase 2)
- Dependency graph (phase 2)

## Scope invariants (for this phase)
- Enforce zero-write in code: no code path calls `br` with any verb other than read verbs
- Bead-creation UI explicitly disabled
- Audit log schema exists but has no writes

## References
- Plan §6 Phase 1 — deliverables and success criteria
- Plan §4 — component architecture
- Plan §5 — data flows
- Notes: all four files in `docs/notes/` apply

## Closing criteria
All child beads closed + success criteria verified in CI against `testrepo/` fixture.

title: Scaffold Rust crate workspace + embedded-static-assets axum setup
description:
## Task
Initialize the Rust workspace for HOOP. Single binary crate. `axum` for HTTP/WS, embedded static assets for the web client.

## Why
Everything downstream depends on this. Target: `cargo build` produces a binary that starts, opens a listen socket, and serves a health endpoint.

## Acceptance
- `cargo new --bin hoop` equivalent structure in place
- `axum` + `tokio` + `tracing` dependencies pinned
- Embedded static asset module stubbed (phase 1 UI will ship here)
- `hoop serve` subcommand starts and listens
- `/healthz` returns 200

## Notes
- Crate layout: workspace with `hoop-cli`, `hoop-daemon`, `hoop-schema`, `hoop-ui` (embedded)
- Follow Rust edition 2021+
- Cargo.lock committed

## Plan reference
§7 Tech decisions, §4.1 Daemon subcommands

title: Tag-join resolver: extract [needle:<worker>:<bead>:<strand>] prefix
description:
## Task
Every bead dispatch prefixes the first user message with `[needle:<worker>:<bead-id>:<strand>]`. The resolver extracts this on parse and establishes the session → bead mapping.

## Why
Core mechanism for joining CLI session transcripts to beads. Without it, HOOP can't show "which conversation produced this bead."

## Acceptance
- Regex validated and tested against fixtures (all four adapters)
- Missing tag → session classified as `operator` or `ad-hoc`, not auto-joined
- Malformed tag logged at warn, treated as missing
- Binding emitted as `session_bound` event (dual-identity invariant §B1)

## Plan reference
§5.1 Data flows (tag-join arrow), notes/interop-with-needle.md Hook 1, notes/reference-feature-inventory.md §1

title: session_bound event emitted on first (bead_id, provider_session_id) meeting (idempotent)
description:
## Task
Tag-join resolver emits `session_bound { bead_id, provider_session_id, ts }` EXACTLY ONCE per (bead_id, session_id) pair. Subsequent appearances are no-ops.

## Why
Principle 4: dual-identity in schema. §B1 in prior-art — retrofitting dual-identity is where prior art "bled for weeks."

## Acceptance
- Resolver emits session_bound on first join
- Replay a fixture twice: exactly one session_bound in output
- Both IDs indexed for lookup

## Plan reference
§3.4, notes/orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §B1

title: Implement local Unix-socket control plane at ~/.hoop/control.sock
description:
## Task
The `hoop serve` daemon exposes a local Unix-domain socket at `~/.hoop/control.sock` for other `hoop` subcommands to communicate with it. All CLI clients talk to the daemon through this socket, never directly to state.

## Why
Architectural principle: one long-lived daemon, everything else is a client. Control socket decouples CLI tooling from daemon internals.

## Acceptance
- Socket created on daemon start with user:group permissions
- Simple request/response protocol (JSON lines)
- `hoop status` client works against running daemon
- Socket cleaned up on graceful shutdown
- Permission: only same OS user can connect

## Plan reference
§4.1 Daemon subcommands (note: "other subcommands are clients over Unix socket"), §13 Security (UDS with same user:group)

title: Schema crate: JSON Schema draft-07 source of truth + Rust/TS codegen
description:

Create the `hoop-schema/` crate. JSON Schema files are source of truth; Rust types codegen via `typify`; TS types codegen via `json-schema-to-typescript`. Every record includes `schema_version: "1.0.0"`.

## Acceptance
- At least these schemas: WS messages, UI state, projects.yaml, config.yml, audit row, capacity-account, fleet.db tables (Stitch, Pattern, Reflection rows for forward compat)
- Codegen runs in cargo build.rs (Rust) and pre-build (TS)

description:
## Task
Submit a one-line PR (or equivalent coordination) to NEEDLE: prepend dispatched prompts with `[needle:<worker>:<bead-id>:<strand>]`. That tag is how HOOP joins CLI session transcripts to beads.

## Acceptance

title: NEEDLE hook #2: events.jsonl append helper + heartbeat thread
description:
## Task
Also coordinated with NEEDLE: ensure each worker emits events to `.beads/events.jsonl` and heartbeats to `.beads/heartbeats.jsonl`. Standardize the event shape.

## Acceptance
- Event append helper in NEEDLE worker code (or confirmed already present)
- Heartbeat thread per worker emits every ~10s
- Event shapes match HOOP's parser expectations (documented schema)
- Integration test in HOOP's `testrepo/` validates

## Plan reference
Notes/interop-with-needle.md (Hooks 2 & 3)

title: Worker spawn-ack mechanism (each worker writes ~/.hoop/workers/<name>.ack)
description:
## Task
Each worker, on startup, writes an ack file at `~/.hoop/workers/<name>.ack` to prove successful spawn. HOOP verifies within a grace window (~10s); missing ack = spawn failed.

## Why
Prior-art lesson: tmux send-keys can silently truncate commands; `spawned successfully` can be a lie. Verify by confirmation, not by tmux return code. (§M5)

## Note
In HOOP's final scope, HOOP doesn't spawn workers. This task ensures that WHEN NEEDLE spawns a worker, HOOP can detect spawn success vs silent failure via the ack file.

## Acceptance
- Ack file location and format documented
- HOOP watches for acks and surfaces missing ones as alerts
- NEEDLE-side support (hook) for writing the ack on worker boot

## Plan reference
Notes/orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §M5 Silent tmux-orphan channels

title: Graceful shutdown: SIGTERM → grace → SIGKILL; socket cleanup; state flush
description:
## Task
Daemon handles SIGTERM with grace period, flushes in-flight state to disk, closes socket, exits. Hard SIGKILL backstop after grace.

## Acceptance
- SIGTERM triggers shutdown sequence
- Active WS clients notified (close frame)
- In-flight event-tailer state flushed
- fleet.db checkpointed (SQLite WAL)
- Socket cleaned up
- Exit in <5s under normal load

## Plan reference
Notes/orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §C2 (zombie processes), §3.9 (HOOP dies nothing else notices)

title: Zero-write invariant guard (code path analysis + runtime assertion)
description:
## Task
Enforce that phase 1 code has no `br create` / `br close` / `br update` / `br release` call path. Compile-time enforcement via feature flag; runtime assertion as belt-and-suspenders.

## Why
Phase 1 is strictly read-only. A stray write path that ships breaks the "HOOP is a convenience, not a dependency" invariant.

## Acceptance
- `cargo check --features=zero-write-v01` enforces no write-verb calls reachable
- Runtime check panics early if a write attempt happens (should never trigger)
- CI test fails on write-verb call in phase-1 code paths

## Plan reference
§3.8 Principle (br create is HOOP's only write), §6 Phase 1 deliverable 7 (zero-write invariant)

title: br version-pin check at startup (refuse to start on incompatible br)
description:
## Task
On daemon start, run `br --version` and compare against the pinned minimum version from `hoop-schema/br-compat.toml`. On mismatch, refuse to start with a clear diagnostic.

## Acceptance
- Pinned minimum version in a version file
- `br --version` parsed and compared
- Mismatch diagnostic includes exact expected version
- `--allow-br-mismatch` flag for dev override (documented)

## Plan reference
§2.1 br dependency, §6 Phase 1 deliverable 8
close_reason: Implemented br version-pin check at daemon startup. br --version parsed and compared against pinned minimum from br-compat.toml (0.4.0). Daemon refuses to start on mismatch. --allow-br-mismatch flag available for dev override.

title: Compile-fail trybuild suite for br_verbs.rs (existing code → tracked bead)
description:
## Task
Existing file `tests/compile_fail_create_only.rs` uses trybuild to assert non-create br verbs (claim/close/update/release) cannot be imported from outside test-only modules. Tie this file to a bead so refactors don't silently weaken the invariant.

## Acceptance
- Trybuild suite runs in CI
- Every br verb except `create` produces compile-fail fixture
- Weakening the test is caught in review

## Plan reference
§3 principle 8, §6 Phase 1 deliverable 7

title: docs/needle-hooks.md — canonical contract for all NEEDLE-side hooks

1. Prompt prefix tag `[needle:<worker>:<bead>:<strand>]`
2. `spawned-by: <operator-stitch-id>` marker
3. Stitch-label propagation on follow-up beads

§6 Phase 1 deliverable 5, notes/interop-with-needle.md

CI parses `docs/needle-hooks.md` code fences. Validates each JSON example against the corresponding hoop-schema. Doc drift fails CI.

## Why

§5.1

title: Implement hoop audit — dependency + environment check
description:
## Task
CLI subcommand + daemon-startup check that validates HOOP's runtime prerequisites and reports any failure with the exact command to fix it.

## Checks
- `br` in PATH (`br --version` ≥ pinned minimum)
- `tmux` in PATH
- `.beads/` accessible for each registered project
- CLI session directories readable (`~/.claude/projects/`, `~/.codex/sessions/`, etc.)
- Tailscale interface available (optional — warn only)
- Disk space sufficient (≥ 1GB in `~/.hoop/`)
- systemd user-scope enabled (optional — warn only)

## Why
From prior-art analysis: 90% of "doesn't work" reports are missing dependencies. Surface at startup with the fix command inline.

## Acceptance
- `hoop audit` runs all checks and reports
- Daemon refuses to start (or starts with degraded features flagged) on critical failures
- Each failure includes the fix command verbatim
- Output machine-readable via `--json`

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 1 deliverables #10, §12 Onboarding (dependency check is first step of `hoop init`)
close_reason:
Implemented `hoop audit` CLI subcommand and daemon startup check with all required validations:

**CLI command:**
- `hoop audit` — runs all checks with human-readable output
- `hoop audit --json` — machine-readable JSON output
- `hoop audit --strict` — skip optional checks (Tailscale, systemd)

**Checks implemented:**
1. br in PATH with minimum version (0.4.0) — CRITICAL
2. tmux in PATH — CRITICAL
3. .beads/ accessibility for registered projects — CRITICAL
4. CLI session directories readability — WARNING
5. Tailscale interface available — WARNING
6. Disk space sufficient (>= 1GB) — CRITICAL
7. systemd user-scope enabled — WARNING

**Daemon integration:**
- Startup check runs automatically before server starts
- Critical failures block daemon startup with clear error message
- Warnings logged but allow startup with degraded features

**Each failure includes:**
- Clear description
- Exact command to fix (verbatim)

Tests pass: version comparison, audit report success/failure modes.

title: parse_jsonl_safe helper + ~/.hoop/quarantine/ directory for bad lines
description:
## Task
Every JSONL reader (events, heartbeats, session tailers, config) uses one helper:
1. Try parse each line
2. Schema-validate on success
3. Route bad lines to `~/.hoop/quarantine/<ts>-<reason>/` with source file + offset
4. Emit daily quarantine summary metric

## Why
Prior art: one corrupt JSONL line took whole dashboards down (§L3). Cheap helper prevents whole class of outages.

## Acceptance
- Shared helper in hoop-daemon/src/parse_jsonl_safe.rs
- All JSONL readers migrated to it
- Integration test: inject bad line → quarantine populated → reader continues

## Plan reference
notes/orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §L3

title: Line-buffered NDJSON reader with split-chunk fuzz test
description:
## Task
Shared line-buffered reader that accumulates partial lines across OS-read boundaries. Fuzz test: split a known-good fixture at every possible offset, assert parse completeness.

## Why
§F1 in prior-art notes: stream-JSON output chunked at non-boundary offsets crashes naive `JSON.parse`. "Cheap to do right; expensive to patch later."

## Acceptance
- Helper handles any byte-boundary split point
- Fuzz test covers all offsets in a multi-line fixture
- Memory bounded (partial line length < 1MB)

## Plan reference
notes/orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §F1

title: Workspace path canonicalization at ingestion (realpath; store raw + canonical)
description:
## Task
Every ingested path (project registration, session cwd, bead workspace) goes through `std::fs::canonicalize`. Store BOTH raw and canonical. Joins by canonical; display raw.

## Why
§J2: same workspace appearing as `/home/coding/X` and `/Users/coding/X` (symlinks, alt mounts) → duplicate projects, double-counted costs, collision false-negatives.

## Acceptance
- projects.yaml entries carry both fields
- Session-tailer joins by canonical
- Duplicate-path detection warns on ingest
- Symlink fixtures tested

## Plan reference
notes/orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §J2

title: Add .gitignore at repo root (Rust target/, node_modules, dotenv, OS cruft)
description:
## Task
Create `.gitignore` at repo root covering:
- Rust build artifacts: `target/`, `**/*.rs.bk`, `Cargo.lock` debate (commit for binaries, ignore for libraries — HOOP is a binary so COMMIT)
- Node: `node_modules/`, `dist/`, `.vite/`
- IDE: `.vscode/`, `.idea/`
- OS: `.DS_Store`, `Thumbs.db`, `*~`
- Env/secrets: `.env`, `.env.local`, `*.local.yaml`
- HOOP-local: `~/.hoop/` is outside repo, but tests may create `.hoop/` — ignore it
- testrepo/ quarantine + stub artifacts where local

## Acceptance
- `.gitignore` in place
- `git status` after fresh `cargo build` + `pnpm build` shows no untracked artifacts
- Secrets glob coverage verified

## Plan reference
§13 security (prevent accidental secret commits)

title: hoop projects CLI — add/list/remove (single workspace in phase 1)
description:
## Task
CLI subcommands for managing the project registry. Phase 1 supports single-project; phase 2 expands to multi-project scan and multi-workspace.

## Commands
- `hoop projects add <path>` — register a workspace by path
- `hoop projects list` — list registered projects
- `hoop projects remove <name>` — remove a project
- `hoop projects show <name>` — details of one project

## Why
The operator needs to tell HOOP what to observe. CLI-based for phase 1; UI-based in phase 2.

## Acceptance

- Add path validation: path exists, contains `.beads/`

title: Event tailer: watch .beads/events.jsonl per project (notify crate)
description:
## Task
Implement the event tailer. Watches `.beads/events.jsonl` for the registered workspace with the `notify` crate. Emits parsed events onto an internal broadcast channel.

## Why
The event log is the authoritative source for what NEEDLE workers are doing. HOOP's projections are derived from it.

## Acceptance
- `notify` watcher survives log rotation (handle file-moved events)
- Parser uses line-buffered NDJSON with partial-line carry-over (critical — see orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §F1)
- Malformed lines logged with `warn`, never silent-dropped (§M1)
- Unknown event types emit a progress event + increment `hoop_unknown_event_total` metric
- Replay-from-start on daemon restart reconstructs full state

## Plan reference
§4.3 Per-project runtime, §5.1 Data flows, §5 Principles (events authoritative)

title: Heartbeat monitor: watch .beads/heartbeats.jsonl; compute worker liveness
description:
## Task
Parse heartbeat lines from `.beads/heartbeats.jsonl`. Maintain per-worker `last_heartbeat_at` projection. Combine with `kill -0 pid` from event log's `started` rows to compute liveness.

## Why
Liveness = process + heartbeat, never file. Rule from prior-art analysis (§C1 content-aware heartbeat, §A4 ghost liveness).

## Acceptance
- Heartbeats parse without silent-drop
- Worker liveness computed as `pid_alive AND heartbeat_fresh(≤ 2×heartbeat_interval)`
- Stale workers (heartbeat old + PID alive) flagged as "hung"
- Dead workers (PID gone) flagged as "dead"
- Liveness derivation is pure — no file writes

## Plan reference
§3.2 Principle (liveness = process, never file), §4.3 Per-project runtime, notes/orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §A4, §C1

title: Session tailer: Claude Code adapter (~/.claude/projects/)
description:
## Task
Implement the Claude Code session tailer. Discovers `.jsonl` session files under `~/.claude/projects/<hash>/`, parses them into canonical `ParsedSession` records.

## Discovery pattern
- Two-phase: stat everything + sort by mtime, then parse in parallel (concurrency ~16, batch size 100)
- 5-second background poll detects external edits
- Bootstrap interceptor aliases newly-found files back to existing session IDs to prevent duplicates

## Acceptance
- Discovers all Claude Code sessions under the target dir
- Parses usage fields (message.usage: input/output/cache-read/cache-write)
- Progressive streaming to broadcast channel (batched `conversations_updated`)
- Line-buffered parser handles split chunks (§F1)
- Filter-by-cwd to scope sessions to the registered project

## Plan reference
§4.3 Per-project runtime, §5.1 Data flows, notes/reference-feature-inventory.md §1, notes/orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §A1-A4, §B3
close_reason:

- Parses usage fields (input/output/cache-read/cache-write tokens)

Also fixed line-buffered parser logic in events.rs and sessions.rs to correctly distinguish between partial and malformed lines based on ending bracket check before length threshold.

title: Phase 2: Multi-project + marquee observability features (v0.2)
description:
# Phase 2: Multi-project observability + cost/capacity + visual debug (v0.2)

**Version target:** v0.2 | **Timeline:** +12 weeks

## Goal
One HOOP serves every project on the host. Cross-project dashboards, cost and capacity visibility (read-only — no enforcement), visual debug of what workers did per bead. Four marquee features land alongside: Stitch + Pattern layer, Stitch-Provenance Code Archaeology, Stitch Net-Diff Viewer, Cost-Anomaly with Fix Lineage.

## Why this phase exists
Multi-project correctness is the primary design pressure of HOOP. Session discovery has to be scoped per project or queries return soup. Cost has to bucket per project. The Stitch abstraction must exist before any write path is added so bead-creation can target Stitches, not raw beads.

## What's in scope
- Project registry (multi-workspace support: project may span several repos)
- Per-project runtime isolation (failure in one doesn't cascade)
- Fleet-of-fleets dashboard + project detail views
- Cost panel (observation only; no enforcement, no routing)
- Capacity visibility (per-account 5h/7d utilization; no throttling)
- Visual debug panel (per-bead prompts/tools/state)
- Collision detector, stuck detector
- **Marquee #1:** Stitch abstraction layer (foundational)
- **Marquee #1b:** Pattern layer (optional cross-project grouping)
- **Marquee #2:** Stitch-Provenance Code Archaeology (git blame + Stitch attribution)
- **Marquee #3:** Stitch Net-Diff Viewer (aggregate diff across a Stitch cluster)
- **Marquee #4:** Cost-Anomaly with Fix Lineage

## Non-goals
- Still read-only on bead state; no `br create` from HOOP yet (phase 4)
- No steering of workers, ever
- Capacity displayed, never enforced

## Critical design anchors
- Stitches are conversations (not work items)
- Stitches descend into obscurity by recency (no closed state)
- Patterns cross projects; Stitches don't
- Multi-workspace projects are first-class

## References
- Plan §6 Phase 2
- Plan §1.6 (Stitch + Pattern hierarchy)
- Plan §4.2 (project registry, multi-workspace)
- Plan §4.7 (Stitch / Pattern / Reflection services schema)
- Notes: architecture-patterns.md, orchestrator-problems-and-solutions.md §A, §B, §C, §M

## Closing criteria
- Every phase-2 child bead closed
- `hoop projects scan ~/` registers every `.beads/` workspace in one command
- Cost figures match `br` within ±2%, capacity meters within ±5% of `/status`
- Net-Diff correctly assembles a 5-bead / 11-commit cluster
- Cost anomaly flags a 3σ test case
- UI dashboards contain zero bead IDs by default


```yaml
projects:
  - name: ibkr-mcp
    path: /home/coding/ibkr-mcp  # role defaults to 'primary'
```

title: Three-timer stuck detector: idle + max_runtime + content_seen_grace with saw_content flag
description:
## Task
Refactor stuck detector to use three distinct timers:
1. `idle_timeout` — no events at all
2. `max_runtime` — hard ceiling on total duration
3. `content_seen_grace` — extended idle tolerance once real content appeared

Alert events include `saw_content: bool`.

## Why
§C1 / highest-leverage #5: one timer is wrong. Single-timer alerts fire during legitimate tool-use silence → false positives → operator fatigue → real alerts ignored.

## Acceptance
- Three timer config options per worker-type
- Alert distinguishes silence-before-first-content from silence-after
- Test: synthetic tool-use silence doesn't trigger stuck alert

title: Stitch status derivation: pure function from linked beads + activity
description:
## Task

## Rules

- `Awaiting Review` — review-kind linked beads open
- `Quiet N days` — no activity for N days (N shown to user)

## Why
Stitches descend into obscurity (Reddit model); status is a hint, not a lifecycle terminus. (§1.6, §8.14 non-goal)

## Acceptance
- Pure function; property test: same inputs → same output
- Status derivation: 10ms max for a Stitch with 20 linked beads
- Backward-compat: derivation doesn't require phase 4+ data

## Plan reference
§1.6 Stitch activity indicators, §4.7 Stitch status
close_reason:
Implemented pure function for Stitch status derivation in hoop-daemon/src/stitch_status.rs

Summary:
- Pure function StitchContext::derive_status() computes status from linked beads + activity
- Status rules: In Progress (claimed bead OR recent streaming), Awaiting Review (open review beads), Quiet N days (no activity)
- Comprehensive tests: property test (same inputs → same output), performance test (< 10ms for 20 beads), backward compatibility tests
- Never stored; computed on read per §1.6, §4.7

Note: Codebase has unrelated compilation errors in other files (supervisor.rs, capacity.rs, events.rs) that prevent cargo test from running, but stitch_status.rs itself is complete and correct.

title: Pattern status state-machine: legal transition matrix + property test
description:
## Task
Document legal Pattern status transitions. Property test rejects illegal moves.

## Transition rules
- `Planned → Active | Abandoned` (not → Done)
- `Active → Blocked | Done | Abandoned`
- `Blocked → Active | Abandoned`
- `Done → Active` (explicit reactivate only)
- `Abandoned → Active` (explicit reactivate only)
- Self-transitions allowed (update fields, no status change)

## Why
§4.7: illegal transitions corrupt audit + aggregates.

## Acceptance

- Property test covers all transition pairs
- Illegal transitions rejected at schema boundary

## Plan reference
§4.7, §1.6

title: Pattern-nesting cycle guard (reject parent-link that would form cycle)
description:
## Task
On `set-parent` for a Pattern: walk ancestry chain. Reject if the new parent is a descendant of the current node.

## Why
§1.6: "Parent-Pattern nesting supports epic-in-epic." Cycles break aggregations + render.

description:
# Phase 3: File browser + artifact preview + multimodal (v0.3)

**Version target:** v0.3 | **Timeline:** +16 weeks

## Goal
Operator can browse any project's files, preview code / docs / images / media with syntax highlighting, and supply multimodal input (text / image / audio / video) for Stitch drafting and agent conversations. Two capture surfaces: Dictated Notes (pure note-taking) and Voice/Screen Work Capture (drafts a Stitch).

## Why this phase exists
Humans think with their hands in the code and their voice while walking. A text-only UI misses both. The dictation surface specifically — audio + transcript as first-class entities — captures *why* decisions were made at the moment they were made, which is the most valuable record of real work.

## Scope
- Per-project file browser (tree, filter, mtime/size/git-status)
- Syntax highlighting (server `syntect` + client Shiki)
- Non-text preview: images, PDFs, audio, video, binary hex dump
- Artifact-aware links (bead view → files touched → file browser at right revision)
- Multimodal input to Stitch drafts + agent chat
- Streaming upload with progress + resumability
- Path-sensitive routing (drag file into draft → picks up path + revision + snippet)
- **Marquee #5:** Dictated Notes (first-class conversation entity; audio + Whisper transcript in project timeline)
- **Marquee #6:** Voice / Screen Work Capture (builds on Notes; synthesizes Stitch draft)

## Non-goals
- File editing via UI (read-only)
- Rich cloud-sync of attachments (local only; S3 backup in phase 6 covers archival)

## Key constraints
- Mobile UX matters here — phone-ADB dictation is a primary use case
- Streaming content in a separate reactive atom (in-flight isolation rule)
- Attachment size limits and storage layout matter: audio grows fast

## References
- Plan §6 Phase 3
- Plan §21 (Mobile UX)
- Plan §18 (Privacy & redaction applies heavily here)

## Closing criteria
- File browser <1s directory-expand on 20k-file repo
- Syntax highlighting for Rust/TS/Python/Go/Clojure/YAML/TOML/Markdown/Shell/SQL/Dockerfile
- Image/audio/video preview in Safari/Chrome/Firefox
- 10MB image attachment stored and referenced in a Stitch draft
- End-to-end voice capture on Pixel 6 → transcribed Note in project timeline <60s

title: Phase 4: Stitch creation interface (v0.4)
description:
# Phase 4: Stitch creation interface — HOOP's single write path (v0.4)

**Version target:** v0.4 | **Timeline:** +22 weeks

## Goal
HOOP gains its single write path: creating Stitches (backed by `br create`) via UI form, chat draft, and templates / bulk. Every `br create` is audited, previewed, and decomposes operator intent into the right beads with the right labels. Three marquee features land: "What Will This Take?" preview, Already-Started Detection, Stitch Replay from Failure Point.

## Why this phase exists
The operator's write surface. Until now HOOP is an observer; phase 4 makes it useful as a drafter. The "one write path" invariant means phase 4 is simultaneously the riskiest (it's the only place HOOP touches the queue) and the most constrained (everything happens via `br create` with labels).

## Scope
- Form-based Stitch draft (target project, title, description, kind, deps, labels, attachments, preview)
- Template library (`~/.hoop/templates/` and `<project>/.hoop/templates/`)
- Submit flow: draft → preview → `br create --json` with `stitch:<id>` label → audit row → event → UI redirect
- Chat-driven drafting (lightweight, pre-agent; Haiku-class or rule-based)
- Bulk draft (paste markdown list, split into previewable drafts; hard cap 50)
- Audit trail with `actor` + `source` fields
- **Marquee #7:** "What Will This Take?" Preview (cost p50/p90, duration, risk, conflicts — BEFORE submit)
- **Marquee #8:** Already-Started Detection (semantic pre-dedup across open Stitches)
- **Marquee #9:** Stitch Replay from Failure Point (reconstruct state; resume as new bead or continue in agent)

## Explicit non-actions
- HOOP does NOT `close`, `update`, `claim`, `release`, or any other `br` verb beyond `create`
- Strand is never a routing factor (strands are worker-immutable; the dry-run matches by adapter/model/historical-fit only)

## Critical design anchors
- Every bead HOOP creates carries `stitch:<stitch-id>` label
- Every `br create` produces an audit row in `fleet.db`
- Preview flow is gated — no silent submits
- Bulk ops have a hard ceiling with explicit override

## References
- Plan §6 Phase 4
- Plan §2.1 (`br` dependency — HOOP shells out, never touches `.beads/` directly)
- Plan §4.7 (Stitch service schema)

## Closing criteria
- Form-drafted Stitch appears in NEEDLE's queue and a worker claims it
- Audit row exists for every HOOP-created bead
- Chat-driven drafting produces reasonable first drafts
- Bulk splits 10-item markdown into 10 previewable drafts
- Dry-run estimates within p50/p90 bands for 80% of closed Stitches after 30 days
- Already-Started catches synthetic cross-project duplicate with >95% recall
- Replay reconstructs failure moment and resumes successfully

## Acceptance

title: Stitch Replay: reconstruct failure state + resume-as-new-bead OR continue-in-agent
description:
## Task
When a Stitch's linked bead fails, offer two resume options:
1. **Resume as new bead** — HOOP creates a new bead with same `stitch:<id>` label, pre-populated with "pick up from step N" prompt + reconstructed context.
2. **Continue in agent** — (phase 5) the agent inherits the reconstructed state in its chat.

Reconstruction sources: NEEDLE events, CLI session JSONL, worktree git state at time of failure (via git stash-create hook from NEEDLE).

## Acceptance
- Reconstruction completes in <10s for a 30-minute-work-session failure
- Resume-as-new-bead tested: new bead picks up correctly; old failed bead stays closed as history
- Continue-in-agent: agent sees reconstructed state via MCP context (phase 5)
- NEEDLE hook: `git stash create` on bead failure, stash SHA recorded in events

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 4 marquee #9

title: NEEDLE hook #5: git stash-create on bead failure (for Stitch Replay)
description:
## Task
Coordinated with NEEDLE: on bead failure, NEEDLE runs `git stash create` in the worker's worktree and records the stash SHA in the failure event. HOOP uses this for Stitch Replay reconstruction.

## Acceptance
- NEEDLE PR merged
- stash-create is fast (<2s) and non-destructive
- Stash SHAs accessible via `git stash show` for Replay reconstruction
- Graceful degradation if stash fails (Replay still offers reconstruction from events + transcripts, just without worktree state)

## Plan reference
Notes/interop-with-needle.md, §6 Phase 4 marquee #9

- Runtime guard rejects non-`create` br invocations

4. Inserts audit row with actor + source

Arch-patterns: reject paths are least-tested code. Reusing the happy path (receive authoritative state) keeps them honest.

## Acceptance
- Reject scenarios in tests: field validation fail, auth fail, contention
- All produce error + full rebroadcast
- Client's reducer unchanged for reject vs accept

title: Phase 5: The human-interface agent (v0.5)
description:
# Phase 5: Human-interface agent — the conversational handle (v0.5)

**Version target:** v0.5 | **Timeline:** +28 weeks

## Goal
Seat the human-interface agent: a persistent cross-project conversational partner that reviews artifacts, answers questions, and drafts Stitches. The operator's primary interface to HOOP once this lands. Plus three marquee features: Morning Brief, Cross-Project Stitch Propagation, Reflection Ledger.

## Why this phase exists
Until phase 5, HOOP is a good dashboard. Phase 5 makes it a coordinator. The agent is the difference between "see fleets" and "talk to a coordinator who helps run fleets." Without it, HOOP is valuable but not transformative.

## Scope
- Persistent Claude Code (or any adapter) agent session
- MCP server exposing HOOP's read APIs + one write (`create_stitch`)
- Agent tool belt — one write, many reads (see non-actions below)
- Notification channel (agent receives events, decides what to surface)
- Operator ↔ agent chat pane (primary UI surface of v0.5)
- Agent-off switch (HOOP works without agent enabled)
- Audit trail: `actor: hoop:agent:<session>` on every agent-drafted Stitch
- **LLM-agnostic:** Claude Code / Anthropic API / ZAI+GLM / any NEEDLE-compatible adapter via `config.yml`
- **Marquee #10:** Morning Brief (autonomous; structured briefing + pre-drafted Stitches + one headline)
- **Marquee #11:** Cross-Project Stitch Propagation (sibling-project detection)
- **Marquee #12:** Reflection Ledger (HOOP learns from repetition; proposes rules)

## Agent tool belt (canonical)
- `create_stitch(project, title, description, kind, attachments)` — the one write
- `find_stitches`, `read_stitch`, `find_beads` (expert), `read_bead` (expert)
- `read_file(project, path, revision)`, `grep(project, pattern)`
- `search_conversations(query, project?)`
- `summarize_project(project)`, `summarize_day()`
- `escalate_to_operator(message)` — UI banner; no auto-actions
- **Forbidden:** `launch_fleet`, `stop_fleet`, `release_claim`, `boost_priority`, `close_stitch`, `close_bead`

## Critical design anchors
- Lazy context (§3.12) — thin index by default, details via tool calls
- Stitch drafts always through preview flow (§3.10 read-first default)
- Hard ceilings (max_attempts, max_review_rounds, cycle_budget) enforced at draft time
- Cross-project chat requires explicit project targeting or UI confirmation
- If Mayor is said anywhere in code, UI, or docs: remove — the role is "human-interface agent"

## References
- Plan §6 Phase 5
- Plan §1.6 (Stitches and Patterns)
- Plan §4.7 (Reflection Ledger schema)
- Plan §13 (Security — MCP socket binding)

## Closing criteria
- Agent session survives `systemctl restart hoop` with context intact
- Cross-project summary correct in Stitch language
- Agent never performs a worker action
- Morning Brief produces useful daily summary with a correctly-scoped pre-drafted Stitch
- Cross-project propagation hits a real sibling across 3+ projects
- Reflection proposals flag repeated instructions and are operator-approvable

title: Agent adapter abstraction: Claude Code / Anthropic API / ZAI+GLM / plug-compatible

## Adapter interface

- `resume_session(SessionId) → Session`

- `close_session(session)`

## Acceptance

- Adapter switch requires no code change
- Event shape identical across adapters (test fixture)

title: Reflection Ledger schema: reflection_ledger table + scope model
description:

fleet.db table:
```
reflection_ledger(id, scope, rule, reason, source_stitches, status,
```

title: Reflection detector: scan closed operator Stitches for repeated patterns
description:
## Task
Async background job. After each closed operator Stitch, scan recent operator Stitches (window configurable, default 30d) for:
- Repeated corrections (same correction, 3+ instances)
- Repeated preferences (same preference stated 3+ times)
- Repeated negatives ("don't do X", 3+ times)
- Repeated approvals of non-obvious choices (same acceptance signal, 3+ times)

Detected patterns become `status=proposed` rows in the Reflection Ledger.

## Acceptance
- Runs within 2 minutes of closure
- Uses small local model for similarity grouping (or lexical if local model unavailable)
- Proposal rate expected: 1-3 per day under normal operator use
- Tested against synthetic repeated-instruction fixtures

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 5 marquee #12

title: Reflection Ledger UI: proposal pane with approve/edit/reject
description:
## Task
Dedicated UI pane listing pending proposals. Each shows: suggested rule, reason, source Stitches (clickable). Actions: approve (optionally edit first), reject (optional reason), defer.

## Acceptance
- Proposals surfaced within minute of detection

title: Audit rows on reflection proposal approve / edit / reject (operator identity)
description:
## Task
Each ledger action (approve, edit-then-approve, reject) produces an audit row tying to source_stitches + rule_id + operator identity. Rejection reason optional but captured.

## Why

## Acceptance

- Edit-then-approve: two rows (edit, approve) linked
- Multi-operator scenario: each operator's decisions attributable

title: Reflection Ledger injection: approved rules → every new agent session's lazy-context index
description:
## Task
On new agent session start, approved rules (scoped to relevant project/Pattern + global) are injected into the lazy-context index. Each rule one line; scope noted. Agent sees them as a "standing instructions" list.

## Acceptance
- Injection respects scope (project-scoped rules only for that project's context)

title: Per-session reflection-injection audit (which rules applied to which turn)
description:

When a Reflection Ledger rule is injected into an agent session, write an `actions` row `{kind: reflection_injected, rule_id, session_id, turn_index}` and increment rule.last_applied + applied_count atomically.

## Why
Plan §4.7 requires "every injection is auditable" — operator must be able to trace what shaped a given response. Without this, the "nothing learned silently" contract is unverifiable.

title: Agent session lifecycle: spawn / persist / attach-on-restart / resume-on-adapter-switch
description:

Persistent agent session hosted by HOOP. Session ID tracked in fleet.db. On daemon restart, HOOP reattaches to the existing session (via adapter's native session store). Adapter switch (e.g. Anthropic outage → ZAI failover) starts a new session but carries forward the Reflection Ledger + recent-activity context.

## Acceptance

description:

title: streamingContentAtom pattern: agent-chat + worker-transcript streaming isolated from committed state
description:
## Task

## Why
Critical arch pattern (§B3 prior-art + arch-patterns "Streaming separation"). Without it: token-stream rerenders whole conversation list; crash mid-turn shows stale partial content.

## Acceptance
- Separate atom family keyed by conversation id
- Committed messages NEVER receive partial tokens
- Test: mid-turn WS disconnect → no partial leak into committed store
- Visible performance: 50+ tokens/sec doesn't jank the UI

title: Phase 6: Operational polish (v0.6)
description:
# Phase 6: Operational polish (v0.6)

**Version target:** v0.6 | **Timeline:** +32 weeks

## Goal
Make HOOP pleasant to run for the long haul. systemd, hot-reload, backups, metrics, Tailscale identity, performance budgets.

## Why this phase exists
A tool the operator doesn't want to babysit is a tool they actually use. Phase 6 is where HOOP graduates from "interesting prototype" to "thing I trust to run unattended."

## Scope
- systemd user service template
- Config hot-reload (§17)
- Log rotation
- `/healthz` + `/readyz`
- Daily `fleet.db` snapshot
- Drop-in binary upgrade flow
- Prometheus `/metrics` (§16)
- Tailscale-aware auth (inherited from environment; §13)
- Performance budget: 20 projects × 5 workers × 300 beads, UI responsive
- Graceful degradation on per-project failures

## Closing criteria
- `systemctl --user restart hoop` resumes state in <5s
- Bad `config.yml` edit rejected; old config keeps running
- One month of normal operation produces <1GB in logs+backups
- Operator identity visible in audit log for every mutation
