title: Log rotation: HOOP logs rotate at 100MB or daily; keep 14 days
description:
## Task
HOOP's own logs rotate via size or time trigger. Journal integration is preferred; fallback to file-based rotation with lumberjack-style library.

## Acceptance
- Default path: `~/.hoop/logs/` for file-based
- Rotation on 100MB or 24h (whichever first)
- Kept for 14 days then deleted
- Log line redaction applied at write time (§18)

## Plan reference
§6 Phase 6 deliverable 3
close_reason:
Log rotation fully implemented for both hoop-daemon and hoop-mcp.

## What was done
- Log rotation module at ~/.hoop/logs/ with hoop.YYYY-MM-DD.log / hoop-mcp.YYYY-MM-DD.log naming
- Rotation triggers: 100MB size OR 24h date boundary (whichever first)
- 14-day retention: cleanup_old_logs() runs on startup and every rotation
- Redaction at write time: 4 regex patterns cover sk- API keys, Bearer tokens, key=value secrets, and JSON secrets
- Fixed a bug: the sk- regex was [a-zA-Z0-9]{20,} which couldnt match real keys with dashes (sk-ant-api03-...), changed to [a-zA-Z0-9_-]{20,}
- 7 unit tests covering redaction and file naming (all passing)
- Daemon mirrors to stdout; MCP mirrors to stderr (stdout reserved for JSON-RPC)

## Retrospective
- What worked: The implementation was already complete and correct - the task was a verification pass. The custom RotatingFileWriter implementing std::io::Write integrates cleanly with tracings MakeWriter trait.
- What didnt: The sk- API key regex had a subtle bug - [a-zA-Z0-9] excluded dashes, which are present in all real Anthropic/OpenAI key formats. The test input happened to contain dashes, which caught it.
- Surprise: Both files were nearly identical (daemon vs MCP) with only the PREFIX constant and console mirror target differing. This is a candidate for deduplication into a shared crate, but the duplication is small enough (228-294 lines) to tolerate for now.
- Reusable pattern: For tracing log rotation, implementing std::io::Write on a struct that tracks size/date and wraps a File is the simplest correct approach - no need for external crates like tracing-appender or lumberjack.

title: Backup service: daily fleet.db snapshot + incremental attachments sync to S3
description:
## Task
Scheduled backup per `config.yml backup.schedule`. Operations:
1. `VACUUM INTO` on fleet.db to temp snapshot
2. zstd-compress
3. Optional age-encrypt (if key in env)

5. Incremental sync of attachments (only new/changed since last success)
6. Write manifest.json tying snapshot together + schema version

## Acceptance
- Schedule honored (cron-format)
- Retry on transient failure (exponential backoff, max 3)
- Last-success timestamp recorded + exposed via metric
- Encryption reliable (age key via env only)

title: Phase 7: Multi-operator (v1.0)
description:
# Phase 7: Multi-operator (v1.0)

**Version target:** v1.0 | **Timeline:** +36 weeks

## Goal
More than one person can use the same HOOP instance cleanly. Roles, identity, per-operator state, optional presence.

## Why this phase exists
v1.0 is the public milestone. Multi-operator is the last substantive capability before the tool is something a small team can adopt.

## Scope
- Roles: viewer (read-only) + drafter (read + create Stitches). Two levels only.
- Tailscale identity-based role assignment (§13, inherited auth)
- Audit log carries real operator identity on every bead creation
- Per-operator UI state (pinned projects, last-opened Stitch, filters)
- Optional presence indicators (privacy-toggleable)
- Stitch draft concurrency (§19): drafts server-persisted from open
- Reflection Ledger concurrency: proposal deduplication
- Public README + examples + user docs

## Closing criteria
- Two operators see consistent state at all times
- Viewer role cannot access bead-creation endpoint at schema boundary
- README enables a stranger to install HOOP against their own NEEDLE workspace in <30 min

title: X: Onboarding & documentation (§12)
description:
# Cross-cutting: Onboarding & documentation

**Plan section:** §12

## Why this is cross-cutting
Onboarding isn't a phase — it lands alongside every new surface. A phase that ships without its onboarding piece ships broken for anyone but the author.

## Three surfaces
1. `hoop init` interactive CLI wizard (dep check → project reg → agent setup → systemd → health)
2. In-UI first-run experience (welcome overlay, guided tour, starter prompts)
3. Progressive capability introduction (tips when features go unused, what's-new on upgrade)

## Specific aids
- Explain-this hover (every non-obvious UI element has a one-liner)
- Dry-run for first Stitch drafts
- Optional sample-Stitches tour project
- Agent pre-priming with operator's actual data

## Repo documentation
- `README.md` at repo root (primary; present)
- `AGENTS.md` at repo root (LLM guide; present)
- `docs/plan/plan.md` (authoritative; present)
- `docs/concepts/` — one-pager per concept (future)
- `docs/operations.md` — systemd/backups/upgrades (future)
- `docs/troubleshooting.md` — mapped to `hoop audit` output (future)

## Principle
Onboarding is progressive — never front-loaded. An operator who only wants to observe their fleet shouldn't need to learn Patterns on day one.

## Closing criteria
All phases have their onboarding deliverables landed. Public README enables <30-min stranger setup.

title: hoop init interactive wizard: dep check + project reg + agent setup + systemd + health
description:

4. systemd install
5. Health check + URL print

## Why

## Acceptance

- Wizard explains the default + override path
- Any S3-compatible endpoint works per §15.2

title: Release playbook in docs/operations.md (tag → build → GitHub Release → Docker Hub)
description:
## Task
Document the release process in docs/operations.md:
1. Update CHANGELOG [Unreleased] → [X.Y.Z]
2. Bump Cargo.toml + hoop-ui package.json versions
3. Commit + tag: `git tag vX.Y.Z && git push origin vX.Y.Z`
4. Argo Workflow triggers on tag push
5. Verify GitHub Release artifacts
6. Verify Docker Hub image pushed
7. Post-release: verify install on a fresh host

Include rollback steps (release a revert patch; binary users `curl` older release).

## Acceptance
- Playbook in docs/operations.md
- Dry-run release cut tested end-to-end
- Post-release verification checklist usable

title: hoop-ci Argo Workflow template in jedarden/declarative-config
description:
## Task
Create `hoop-ci` WorkflowTemplate in `jedarden/declarative-config/k8s/iad-ci/argo-workflows/`. Triggered on push to jedarden/HOOP via webhook. Stages:
1. Checkout
2. cargo build (workspace, release)
3. Tests (cargo test + Playwright)
4. Cross-compile linux-x86_64 binary
5. Publish to GitHub Release on tag push

Mirrors pattern from `needle-ci`, `forge-ci`, `sigil-ci`.

## Why
Plan §7: CI via Argo Workflows on iad-ci. GitHub Actions explicitly disabled in this environment. Can't release without it.

## Acceptance
- Template committed to declarative-config
- ArgoCD sync creates the WorkflowTemplate in iad-ci
- Webhook trigger verified with a dry-run push
- Docker Hub publish to `ronaldraygun/hoop:latest` (plan §12 deployment)

## Plan reference
§7 tech decisions (CI/CD on Argo)
